heroOverview: Basophilia is an absolute basophil count exceeding 100 cells per microlitre of blood — a threshold representing the upper limit of the normal range in most reference laboratories. Basophils are the least numerous circulating white blood cells, normally comprising only 0.5-1% of the leucocyte differential, yet they carry the highest density of IgE receptors of any circulating cell and play a pivotal role in allergic inflammation and anti-parasitic defence. Because basophils are so rare in healthy blood, even a modest percentage elevation translates to a clinically meaningful absolute rise. An elevated basophil count is particularly important as a sentinel sign of myeloproliferative neoplasms: basophilia is present in approximately 50-60% of patients with chronic myeloid leukaemia (CML) at diagnosis and is incorporated into the Sokal and EUTOS prognostic scores. Isolated basophilia in the absence of myeloproliferative disease is usually reactive and carries a benign prognosis.

keyStats.prevalence: Basophilia as an isolated finding occurs in fewer than 1% of routine complete blood counts in the general population; it is present at diagnosis in approximately 50-60% of CML cases
keyStats.demographics: Reactive basophilia occurs at any age; CML-associated basophilia predominantly affects adults aged 50-65
keyStats.avgAge: Median age for CML diagnosis is 55-65 years; reactive basophilia has no age predilection
keyStats.globalCases: CML has an incidence of 1-2 per 100,000 per year worldwide; basophilia from any cause affects a far smaller proportion than eosinophilia

definition: Basophilia (ICD-10: D72.824) is defined as an absolute basophil count (ABC) above 100 cells per microlitre, or a percentage above 1% of the leucocyte differential on a complete blood count. Basophils are bone marrow-derived granulocytes released under the influence of thymic stromal lymphopoietin (TSLP), IL-3, and stem cell factor. Their surface membranes carry high-affinity IgE receptors (FcεRI) and CD123; cross-linking of receptor-bound IgE by antigen triggers rapid degranulation, releasing histamine, heparin, leukotrienes, and IL-4. In allergic disease, this mechanism underlies immediate hypersensitivity responses. In myeloproliferative neoplasms, basophils are part of the malignant clone and their count reflects disease burden. The condition is classified as reactive (secondary) when driven by an identifiable external stimulus, or clonal (primary) when basophil overproduction is driven by a haematopoietic stem cell mutation. Clonal basophilia is a defining feature of CML — characterised by the BCR-ABL1 fusion gene — and is also seen in other myeloproliferative neoplasms. Marked basophilia (above 20% of the leucocyte differential) in CML is associated with blast phase transformation, a haematological emergency.

typesClassification.description: A polyclonal increase driven by allergic disease, hypothyroidism, inflammatory conditions, or certain infections; typically mild (100-300/µL) and resolves when the underlying cause is managed.
Clonal basophil expansion arising from the BCR-ABL1-positive clone; basophilia of any degree in CML reflects disease burden and is a component of prognostic scoring systems.
Polycythaemia vera, essential thrombocythaemia, and primary myelofibrosis can produce moderate basophilia as part of pan-granulocytic expansion.
A rare malignancy — acute or chronic — characterised by predominantly basophilic blast or mature basophil proliferation; requires differentiation from blast phase CML.
Some medications including oestrogen-containing contraceptives and antithyroid drugs (carbimazole) are associated with mild basophilia through unclear mechanisms.

primarySymptoms: Persistent fatigue and generalised weakness that reflects either an underlying myeloproliferative disorder or the systemic inflammatory state driving reactive basophilia.

Pruritus (itching), sometimes intense, caused by histamine release from degranulating basophils; this is especially prominent in polycythaemia vera where aquagenic pruritus after water contact is characteristic.
Urticaria (hives), flushing, or erythema driven by histamine and leukotrienes released from activated basophils in allergic and mast cell-related conditions.
Abdominal discomfort, bloating, or peptic ulcer-like symptoms from histamine stimulating gastric acid secretion — a mechanism analogous to that in mastocytosis.
Splenomegaly causing early satiety and left upper abdominal fullness, characteristic of CML and other myeloproliferative disorders where splenic infiltration by the malignant clone occurs.
Night sweats and unintentional weight loss reflecting the systemic inflammatory and metabolic burden of advanced myeloproliferative disease.
Bone pain, joint aches, and a sense of pressure in the sternum or long bones from marrow expansion by the malignant clone.
Easy bruising or bleeding disproportionate to injury, resulting from platelet dysfunction — common in myeloproliferative neoplasms where qualitatively abnormal platelets impair coagulation.
Pallor and dyspnoea on exertion reflecting normocytic anaemia from marrow infiltration or ineffective erythropoiesis in advanced myeloid disease.
Unexplained fever in acute or accelerated-phase myeloproliferative disease, which can signal blast transformation or concurrent infection in an immunocompromised host.
earlyWarningSigns: An incidental basophil percentage above 1% on a routine full blood count, especially if accompanied by any rise in white cell count or splenomegaly.
New or worsening pruritus — particularly after water exposure — without a skin diagnosis.
Mild, unexplained fatigue with a slightly elevated white cell count in an adult over 50.
A newly palpable spleen tip on physical examination in someone without prior liver or haematological disease.
emergencySigns: Basophilia above 20% of the leucocyte differential in a patient with known CML — suggests blast crisis transformation, a haematological emergency.
Sudden onset of severe headache, visual changes, or stroke symptoms in a patient with elevated platelet count and basophilia — possible thrombotic complication of a myeloproliferative neoplasm.
Acute anaphylaxis with hypotension and urticaria in a patient with high basophil counts and known allergy — emergency adrenaline and airway management.
Acute abdominal pain with fever and leucocytosis in a patient with untreated CML — splenic infarction or rupture must be excluded.

causes.cause: Chronic myeloid leukaemia (CML) and BCR-ABL1 fusion
causes.description: The Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22) produces the BCR-ABL1 fusion protein, a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives autonomous overproduction of myeloid cells including basophils, neutrophils, and eosinophils; basophilia is present in over half of CML diagnoses and its degree reflects clone expansion.

Persistent allergen exposure and elevated IgE drive IL-3 and TSLP secretion, which stimulate basophil production and trafficking from bone marrow to tissues; mild basophilia (100-300/µL) is found in chronic allergic rhinitis, asthma, atopic dermatitis, and food allergy.

A well-recognised but incompletely understood association: hypothyroid states are associated with mild basophilia, likely through altered thyroid hormone modulation of haematopoietic progenitor differentiation, and usually normalises with thyroxine replacement.

Polycythaemia vera, essential thrombocythaemia, and primary myelofibrosis involve pan-myeloid proliferation that includes basophil overproduction; JAK2 V617F mutation-driven signalling enhances granulopoiesis broadly, encompassing the basophil lineage.

Rheumatoid arthritis, inflammatory bowel disease, and certain viral infections produce cytokine environments (elevated IL-3) that modestly increase circulating basophils as a reactive response; counts rarely exceed 500/µL in pure inflammation.

Oestrogen-containing oral contraceptives and ovulation are associated with mild transient basophilia, reflecting oestrogen-mediated upregulation of IL-3 receptor expression on basophil precursors; the mechanism is pharmacological rather than pathological.

riskFactors.description: Myeloproliferative neoplasms, the most clinically significant cause of basophilia, predominantly arise in this age group; the cumulative risk of acquired somatic mutations in haematopoietic stem cells increases with age.
Chronic allergic rhinitis, asthma, or atopic dermatitis maintains a low-grade IL-3 stimulus that can sustain mild reactive basophilia; managing the atopic disease typically normalises the count.
Untreated or undertreated hypothyroidism is independently associated with mild basophilia; replacement therapy with levothyroxine reliably reduces the count.

Patients already diagnosed with CML, polycythaemia vera, or myelofibrosis are at high risk of basophilia as part of their disease course; rising basophil counts in CML signal inadequate disease control or accelerated-phase progression.
Oestrogen can mildly elevate basophil counts through hormonal effects on haematopoiesis; this is benign but should be recognised to avoid unnecessary investigation in young women.
Sustained cytokine release from rheumatoid arthritis, inflammatory bowel disease, or chronic infections provides a low-level IL-3 stimulus maintaining mild reactive basophilia.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 50-70 with myeloproliferative neoplasms, particularly CML
Young and middle-aged adults with chronic atopic conditions (asthma, eczema, allergic rhinitis)
Women using oestrogen-containing contraceptives, in whom transient mild basophilia is a harmless finding
Patients with untreated or inadequately treated hypothyroidism
People with chronic autoimmune diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis or inflammatory bowel disease
diagnosisOverview: Evaluation begins with careful attention to the automated differential on a full blood count. Because basophils are so rare, a percentage above 1% on a manual film review — confirming the automated count — always warrants further assessment. The first step is to determine whether the basophilia is reactive or clonal. A full clinical history covering atopic symptoms, thyroid disease, medication use, and constitutional symptoms (weight loss, night sweats, bone pain, abdominal fullness) guides investigation. Initial blood tests include a thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) level to exclude hypothyroidism, a comprehensive metabolic panel, and a reticulocyte count to assess marrow function. When clonal disease is suspected — particularly if the white cell count is elevated, splenomegaly is present, or the basophilia is above 3-5% — peripheral blood FISH or PCR for the BCR-ABL1 fusion transcript is the single most important test, with a sensitivity exceeding 99% for CML. A JAK2 V617F mutation screen is added when polycythaemia vera or other JAK2-positive MPNs are in the differential. Bone marrow trephine biopsy provides definitive characterisation when molecular tests suggest a myeloid neoplasm, yielding cytogenetics, histology, and fibrosis grading. Serum IgE and specific allergen testing are appropriate when allergy is the suspected driver. The peripheral blood film review is valuable to detect basophils with abnormal granularity, hypersegmented neutrophils, or blast forms that indicate disease phase.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count with manual differential
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the absolute basophil count and percentage; manual review by a morphologist confirms the automated result and assesses for concurrent white cell count elevation, left shift, or blast forms.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw with results typically available within hours; manual film review adds 1-2 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects the Philadelphia chromosome translocation t(9;22) or BCR-ABL1 fusion transcript to diagnose CML; also used to monitor treatment response and detect resistance mutations.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood sample sent to a molecular genetics laboratory; FISH results in 3-5 days, PCR quantification in 3-7 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the JAK2 point mutation present in approximately 95% of polycythaemia vera, 50% of essential thrombocythaemia, and 50% of myelofibrosis cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood-based allele-specific PCR; results in 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for hypothyroidism, a reversible cause of mild basophilia that normalises with thyroxine replacement.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw; results typically available within 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum IgE and allergen-specific IgE panel
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies an allergic aetiology when atopic disease is the suspected driver of reactive basophilia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; total IgE in 24-48 hours. Specific panels may take several days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides definitive characterisation of myeloid neoplasms: quantifies blast percentage, assesses fibrosis grade, and supplies material for cytogenetic and molecular studies.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Short procedure under local anaesthetic at the posterior iliac crest; mild soreness for 1-2 days. Full results in 7-14 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Morphological assessment of all blood cell lineages; identifies basophilic granulation patterns, dysplastic changes, or circulating blasts that indicate disease phase.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: No additional blood draw required if a standard EDTA sample was already taken; a trained morphologist reviews the stained film.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of basophilia is directed entirely at the underlying cause; there is no treatment targeting basophil count in isolation. Reactive basophilia caused by hypothyroidism resolves predictably within weeks of initiating adequate levothyroxine replacement, and the TSH should be re-checked to confirm normalisation. Allergic basophilia is managed with antihistamines, inhaled or intranasal corticosteroids, and, where appropriate, allergen immunotherapy, all of which reduce the cytokine drive maintaining the elevated count. For CML, the standard first-line treatment since 2001 has been tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs): imatinib 400 mg daily produces complete haematological response in more than 95% of patients within 3 months, including rapid normalisation of basophilia. Second-generation TKIs — dasatinib (100 mg daily) and nilotinib (600 mg daily divided) — achieve faster and deeper molecular responses and are used first-line in patients with higher-risk disease or imatinib intolerance. Third-generation asciminib is approved for TKI-resistant or intolerant CML. Rising basophilia during CML treatment is a warning sign of disease progression to accelerated or blast phase, which requires urgent bone marrow reassessment and treatment intensification. For polycythaemia vera and other JAK2-positive MPNs with basophilia, ruxolitinib (a JAK1/2 inhibitor) reduces pan-myeloid proliferation including basophil excess. Response monitoring uses BCR-ABL1 quantitative PCR with milestones defined by ELN (European LeukemiaNet) guidelines at 3, 6, and 12 months.

medicalTreatments.name: Imatinib (first-generation TKI)
medicalTreatments.description: Inhibits BCR-ABL1, PDGFR, and c-KIT; the original BCR-ABL1 inhibitor and standard first-line therapy for CML. Dosed at 400 mg/day orally. Produces rapid normalisation of basophil count along with full haematological response.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete haematological response in more than 95% of CML patients at 3 months; 5-year major molecular response rate approximately 60% in the IRIS trial.

medicalTreatments.name: Dasatinib or nilotinib (second-generation TKIs)
medicalTreatments.description: More potent BCR-ABL1 inhibitors that achieve faster and deeper molecular responses than imatinib and are active against most imatinib-resistance mutations except T315I. Used as first-line alternatives or after imatinib intolerance or resistance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Deep molecular response (BCR-ABL1 <0.01% on international scale) achieved in approximately 40-50% of patients at 5 years, compared to approximately 30% with imatinib.

medicalTreatments.name: Asciminib (STAMP inhibitor)
medicalTreatments.description: A third-generation TKI targeting the ABL1 myristoyl pocket rather than the ATP-binding site; approved for CML after two or more prior TKIs, and for the T315I-mutant disease. Produces responses in heavily pre-treated patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Major molecular response in approximately 25% of CML patients with T315I mutation after prior TKI therapy in the ASCEMBL trial.

medicalTreatments.name: Ruxolitinib (JAK1/2 inhibitor)
medicalTreatments.description: Used for basophilia in polycythaemia vera and myelofibrosis; reduces JAK-STAT-driven pan-myeloid proliferation, lowering granulocyte counts including basophils, reducing spleen size, and improving constitutional symptoms.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete haematological response rate of approximately 21% in PV after hydroxyurea failure (RESPONSE trial); symptom improvement in over 50%.

medicalTreatments.name: Levothyroxine replacement
medicalTreatments.description: For hypothyroid-driven basophilia, restoring euthyroid state with weight-adjusted levothyroxine reliably normalises the basophil count, typically within 4-8 weeks of achieving target TSH.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Basophil count returns to normal in virtually all cases of pure hypothyroid-driven reactive basophilia once TSH is controlled within the reference range.

alternativeTreatments.name: Allergen immunotherapy
alternativeTreatments.description: For atopy-driven basophilia, structured immunotherapy over 3-5 years reduces IgE sensitisation and IL-3-mediated basophil stimulation; not indicated for clonal disease.

alternativeTreatments.name: Antihistamines for symptom control
alternativeTreatments.description: Non-sedating H1 antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine) and H2 blockers reduce histamine-mediated symptoms (pruritus, urticaria) from basophil degranulation while awaiting disease-directed treatment.

whySeeSpecialist: A haematologist should be consulted whenever the absolute basophil count exceeds 200/µL, the basophil percentage is above 2%, or the white cell count is concurrently elevated. Even mild basophilia in an adult with splenomegaly, unexplained weight loss, or bone pain warrants urgent evaluation to exclude CML before attributing the finding to allergy or thyroid disease. Basophilia is one of the few haematological findings where a diagnostic delay of even weeks can allow CML to progress from chronic phase (easily controlled) to accelerated or blast phase (much harder to treat).
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a haematologist or haematological oncologist at a centre with access to BCR-ABL1 molecular monitoring, bone marrow biopsy services, and the full range of TKI options including second- and third-generation agents. Patients with CML should be seen at least at a regional haematology programme that follows ELN response milestones. For purely reactive basophilia, an allergist or endocrinologist can direct care once myeloid neoplasm has been excluded.
preventionStrategies: There is no established way to prevent the chromosomal translocation causing CML-associated basophilia.
Manage hypothyroidism proactively with regular TSH monitoring and dose adjustments; this prevents thyroid-associated basophilia.
Control atopic disease with guideline-directed therapy to reduce the chronic cytokine stimulus driving reactive basophilia.
Attend scheduled haematology monitoring if you have a known myeloproliferative neoplasm; rising basophilia is an early warning of disease progression.
Report any new unexplained fatigue, weight loss, abdominal fullness, or persistent itching to your clinician so blood count evaluation can be arranged promptly.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain thyroid medication adherence if hypothyroidism is contributing to basophilia; missed doses can cause count re-elevation.
Reduce known allergen exposure at home (dust mite, pet dander, food triggers) to lower the allergic drive of reactive basophilia.
Stay well hydrated and protect skin from water-triggered pruritus with emollients if aquagenic itching is a symptom.
Report any new constitutional symptoms — night sweats, weight loss, bone pain — to your haematologist as they may indicate disease progression.
Attend all scheduled blood count and BCR-ABL1 PCR monitoring appointments if receiving TKI therapy for CML.

recommended: Well-balanced diet adequate in protein and micronutrients to support haematopoiesis and treatment tolerance
Adequate fluid intake to support kidney clearance of TKI metabolites
Foods rich in magnesium and calcium if muscle cramps occur on imatinib
avoid: Grapefruit, which inhibits CYP3A4 and can unpredictably raise plasma levels of imatinib and nilotinib
High-histamine foods (aged cheese, fermented foods, alcohol, processed meats) if basophil-driven histamine symptoms are prominent
Ibuprofen and NSAIDs without medical guidance in myeloproliferative neoplasms due to risk of exacerbating platelet dysfunction

exerciseGuidelines: Patients with reactive basophilia due to allergy or hypothyroidism can exercise without restriction, adjusted for their underlying condition. CML patients on TKI therapy often experience fatigue and muscle cramps, particularly early in treatment, but regular moderate aerobic exercise is beneficial and well-tolerated once initial cytopenias stabilise. High-impact contact sports should be discussed with the haematologist in patients with significant splenomegaly due to risk of splenic rupture.
dailyManagement: Take TKI therapy at the same time each day with food to reduce nausea; do not crush imatinib tablets.
Monitor for signs of TKI side effects: oedema, muscle cramps, skin rash, and cytopenias.
Attend scheduled BCR-ABL1 quantitative PCR monitoring every 3 months for the first year of CML therapy to confirm response milestones.
Record any new or worsening symptoms — particularly bone pain, increasing abdominal fullness, or fever — and contact your team promptly.
Maintain thyroid medication consistently if hypothyroidism is contributing to basophilia.
prognosis: The prognosis for basophilia mirrors that of the underlying condition. Reactive basophilia from hypothyroidism or atopic disease is entirely benign and resolves with treatment; it does not progress or cause organ damage. CML, the most important clonal cause, has been transformed by TKI therapy: five-year overall survival now exceeds 90% for chronic-phase CML managed with modern TKIs, compared to approximately 30% in the pre-imatinib era. Treatment-free remission (TFR) — stopping TKIs while maintaining molecular response — is now achievable in approximately 40-50% of patients who reach a sustained deep molecular response. Basophilia above 20% of the differential in CML signals accelerated or blast phase, where median survival without allogeneic transplantation remains significantly shorter. Polycythaemia vera and other MPNs causing basophilia carry variable long-term risks of transformation to myelofibrosis or acute leukaemia, managed with JAK inhibitors and, in selected cases, allotransplantation.
recoveryTimeline: Reactive basophilia from hypothyroidism typically normalises within 4-8 weeks of initiating adequate levothyroxine replacement. Allergic basophilia may take weeks to months to fully resolve with optimal allergen control and anti-inflammatory therapy. In CML, imatinib produces complete haematological response — including basophil normalisation — in most patients within the first 3 months of treatment. Second-generation TKIs achieve this response even faster, typically within 1-2 months of initiating therapy.
complications: Blast crisis in CML — basophilia above 20% is a warning sign of transformation to blast phase, which can be rapid and life-threatening without urgent treatment escalation.
Histamine-mediated complications — severe basophil degranulation can cause haemorrhagic peptic ulcers, pruritus severe enough to impair sleep, and, rarely, anaphylaxis.
Thrombotic events in myeloproliferative neoplasms — pan-myeloid expansion including basophilia in PV and ET raises viscosity and platelet activation, increasing stroke and clot risk.
Splenic complications — massive splenomegaly from CML or myelofibrosis risks splenic infarction or rupture, presenting as acute left-sided abdominal pain.

TKI-related adverse effects — imatinib causes oedema, nausea, and muscle cramps in up to 40% of patients; nilotinib carries cardiovascular and metabolic risks; dasatinib is associated with pleural effusion.

supportResources.name: Leukaemia & Lymphoma Society — CML
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient education on chronic myeloid leukaemia, TKI therapy, and support programmes for patients and carers.

supportResources.name: NIH National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute — Blood Disorders
supportResources.description: Overview of blood cell count abnormalities including basophilia from the US National Institutes of Health.

supportResources.name: Max Foundation — CML Patient Support
supportResources.description: International support organisation providing access to TKI medications and patient support in lower-income countries.

sources.title: Hochhaus A et al. European LeukemiaNet 2020 recommendations for treating CML. Leukemia, 2020
Druker BJ et al. Five-year follow-up of patients receiving imatinib for CML (IRIS trial). NEJM, 2006
Kantarjian H et al. Dasatinib versus imatinib in newly diagnosed CML (DASISION). NEJM, 2010

confusedWithConditions.name: Leukaemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Leukaemia involves malignant expansion of multiple blood cell lines; basophilia is a count elevation in one specific granulocyte type. CML is a leukaemia that commonly presents with basophilia, but isolated basophilia from allergy or thyroid disease is not leukaemia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Eosinophilia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Eosinophilia and basophilia are both granulocyte count elevations but involve different cell types with different drivers; eosinophilia is more commonly reactive from parasites and allergy, while basophilia is more specifically associated with myeloproliferative neoplasms when clonal.

confusedWithConditions.name: Allergic asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Allergic asthma can cause mild reactive basophilia through sustained IL-3 and IgE-mediated signalling, but asthma itself does not cause clinically significant basophilia; a high basophil count warrants investigation beyond the atopic diagnosis.

relatedConditions.name: Leukaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: CML is the most important cause of clonal basophilia; basophil percentage is incorporated into CML prognostic scoring systems and rises with disease progression.

relatedConditions.name: Polycythaemia vera
relatedConditions.relevance: A JAK2-driven myeloproliferative neoplasm in which pan-myeloid expansion includes basophilia; both CML and PV cause elevated granulocyte counts but are distinguished by molecular testing.

relatedConditions.name: Eosinophilia
relatedConditions.relevance: Basophilia and eosinophilia frequently co-occur in CML and in allergic disease, sharing a common cytokine milieu of elevated IL-3 and IL-5 driving both lineages.

relatedConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: A reversible cause of mild reactive basophilia; thyroid function testing should be performed in all patients with unexplained basophilia before assuming a myeloproliferative cause.

faqs.question: What is basophilia?
faqs.answer: Basophilia means the number of basophils — the rarest white blood cell type — is higher than normal in the blood, above 100 cells per microlitre. It can be a harmless reactive response to allergy or thyroid disease, or an early sign of conditions such as chronic myeloid leukaemia.

faqs.question: What causes basophilia?
faqs.answer: The most common causes are chronic myeloid leukaemia (CML), allergic diseases such as asthma and hay fever, hypothyroidism, and other myeloproliferative blood disorders. Oestrogen-containing contraceptives cause a mild transient rise. Identifying the cause requires a full blood count, thyroid test, and sometimes a genetic test for CML.

faqs.question: Is basophilia serious?
faqs.answer: It depends on the cause. Basophilia from thyroid disease or allergy is benign and resolves with treatment. Basophilia from CML is clinically significant because CML requires prompt treatment with tyrosine kinase inhibitors. Any unexplained basophilia above 2% of white cells warrants investigation.

faqs.question: What is the normal basophil count?
faqs.answer: Basophils normally make up 0-1% of circulating white blood cells, corresponding to fewer than 100 cells per microlitre. A count above 100 per microlitre or a percentage above 1% is defined as basophilia. Even a small percentage elevation is meaningful because basophils are normally so rare.

faqs.question: Can basophilia be a sign of leukaemia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Basophilia is present at diagnosis in approximately 50-60% of patients with chronic myeloid leukaemia (CML). In CML, the basophil rise reflects expansion of the malignant clone. When basophilia exceeds 20% of the differential, it can indicate blast phase CML, an emergency. Any unexplained basophilia with an elevated total white cell count should be tested for the CML-defining BCR-ABL1 fusion gene.

faqs.question: How is basophilia treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment targets the cause. CML-associated basophilia responds rapidly to imatinib or second-generation tyrosine kinase inhibitors, normalising within weeks. Hypothyroid basophilia resolves with levothyroxine. Allergic basophilia improves with antihistamines, inhaled steroids, and allergen control. There is no medication that targets basophil count alone.

faqs.question: Does basophilia cause symptoms?
faqs.answer: Reactive basophilia from allergy can contribute to itching, hives, and flushing through histamine release. CML-associated basophilia typically accompanies the CML picture of fatigue, splenomegaly, and weight loss. Isolated mild basophilia from thyroid disease is usually asymptomatic and found incidentally on a blood test.

faqs.question: What tests are needed for basophilia?
faqs.answer: Initial tests include a full blood count with differential, thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), and a review of current medications. If CML is suspected — especially with a high white cell count or splenomegaly — BCR-ABL1 PCR or FISH is the key diagnostic test. Serum IgE testing addresses allergic causes.

faqs.question: Can allergies cause basophilia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chronic allergic conditions such as asthma, eczema, and hay fever maintain elevated IL-3 levels, which stimulate basophil production. The resulting basophilia is usually mild (100-300 per microlitre) and improves when the atopic condition is better controlled.

faqs.question: Does hypothyroidism cause basophilia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hypothyroidism is a recognised cause of mild reactive basophilia through thyroid hormone effects on haematopoietic progenitor cells. The count typically normalises within 4-8 weeks of starting adequate levothyroxine replacement and achieving target TSH levels.

faqs.question: What is the connection between basophilia and chronic myeloid leukaemia?
faqs.answer: In CML, the BCR-ABL1 fusion gene drives autonomous overproduction of all myeloid cells, including basophils. Basophilia is present in more than half of CML diagnoses and is factored into prognostic scores. Rising basophilia during TKI therapy signals inadequate control or disease progression and prompts urgent molecular reassessment.

faqs.question: Can basophilia go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Reactive basophilia caused by a drug, viral illness, or minor inflammatory stimulus can normalise spontaneously within weeks. Hypothyroid and allergic basophilia require treating the underlying condition. CML-associated basophilia does not resolve without TKI therapy and will worsen if untreated.

faqs.question: What does a basophil percentage above 20% mean?
faqs.answer: A basophil percentage above 20% of the white cell differential in a patient with CML is a WHO criterion for accelerated phase disease and may indicate impending blast crisis. This finding requires immediate haematology review, repeat bone marrow assessment, and usually a change in therapy.

faqs.question: Are there foods to avoid with basophilia?
faqs.answer: If basophil-driven histamine release is causing symptoms such as itching or flushing, reducing high-histamine foods — aged cheeses, fermented products, alcohol, processed meats — may help. Grapefruit should be avoided in patients taking imatinib or nilotinib for CML because it interferes with drug metabolism.

faqs.question: How is CML monitored when basophilia is present?
faqs.answer: CML is monitored with BCR-ABL1 quantitative PCR every 3 months for the first year of TKI therapy, following ELN milestone definitions. A rising BCR-ABL1 transcript level, particularly with increasing basophilia, triggers mutation testing for resistance and consideration of switching to a different TKI.

faqs.question: Can children develop basophilia?
faqs.answer: Yes, though it is rare. In children, reactive causes such as viral infections, atopic disease, or inflammatory conditions are the most common reasons. CML is uncommon in children under 18 but occurs. Any child with an unexplained elevated basophil count should be evaluated by a paediatric haematologist.

faqs.question: What blood smear findings are associated with basophilia?
faqs.answer: On a peripheral blood film, basophilia from CML typically shows cells with coarse dark-purple granules, a spectrum of granulocyte maturation stages, and sometimes circulating eosinophils. Blast forms and basophilic blast cells in large numbers indicate blast phase. Reactive basophilia shows morphologically normal basophils without other abnormalities.

faqs.question: Is itching (pruritus) a symptom of basophilia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Basophils release histamine when activated, which causes pruritus, urticaria, and flushing. In polycythaemia vera, characteristic aquagenic pruritus triggered by contact with water is partly driven by basophil and mast cell histamine release. Antihistamines can relieve this while disease-directed treatment addresses the cause.

faqs.question: Can basophilia affect pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Mild basophilia from atopic disease or oestrogen effects is common in pregnancy and is generally benign. CML diagnosed in pregnancy requires careful management because most TKIs are teratogenic; treatment decisions involve haematologists and maternal-fetal medicine specialists, weighing risks and benefits for mother and fetus.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for a high basophil count?
faqs.answer: See a doctor if your basophil count is above 200 per microlitre, the percentage exceeds 2%, or if the elevated count accompanies any of the following: unexplained weight loss, night sweats, bone pain, abdominal fullness, or an elevated total white cell count. These features together require urgent exclusion of CML.

metaDescription: Basophilia is a high basophil count, linked to CML, allergy, or thyroid disease. Learn causes, diagnosis, and treatment options. Doctor-reviewed.
